issues.comment: The second `Objects.requireNonNull` checks `grantType` a second time instead of checking `rawTokenId`. This is a classic copy-paste bug. The error message says "Null rawTokenId not allowed" but it's actually validating `grantType` again. As a result, a `null` value for `rawTokenId` will not be caught by the constructor's validation, and will instead cause a NullPointerException later when `rawTokenId` is used. Fix: Change to `Objects.requireNonNull(rawTokenId, "Null rawTokenId not allowed");`

This line has two bugs: 1) Wrong substring indices: The encoded token format is SS(0-1) + TT(2-3) + GG(4-5) + ':' + rawId. The grant type shortcut occupies indices 4 and 5, so the correct call is `substring(4, 6)`. Using `substring(3, 5)` extracts one char of the token type and one char of the grant type. 2) Inverted logic: The condition returns `false` when the shortcut matches, which is backwards. It should return `false` when the shortcut does not match (i.e., `!equals`). The current logic means: "if the grant type is correct, the token ID is invalid" — the exact opposite of the intent. The combined effect is that the matcher never actually validates the grant shortcut, making all tests that use `isAccessTokenId(...)` pass regardless of which grant type is in the token ID. Fix: `if (!items[0].substring(4, 6).equals(expectedGrantShortcut)) return false;`
